enrichment.short_description: Corteza is an open-source, low-code platform (Apache 2.0) with a built-in CRM module for records-based business apps.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Corteza is a free, open-source low-code platform with a built-in CRM, messaging, and workflow automation, licensed under Apache 2.0.

features.title: Low-code CRM
features.description: Gives a 360-degree overview of leads and accounts, with a module for automating sales and service processes.

features.title: Unified Workspace
features.description: A Google Workspace-style collaboration layer bundled into the platform.

features.title: Enterprise Messaging
features.description: Built-in Slack-style internal messaging for teams.

features.title: Flattened RBAC
features.description: Role-based access control that lets organizations apply complex internal security policies.

features.title: Low-code app builder
features.description: Build and iterate custom records-based business apps beyond CRM.

features.title: Accessibility
features.description: The platform is built to conform to WCAG 2.1 accessibility guidelines.

pros: Fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license with no vendor lock-in
Code is contributed to the Commons Conservancy Software Foundation, so access cannot be revoked by a single vendor
Bundles CRM, internal messaging, and workspace tools in one self-hosted platform
Actively maintained on GitHub with recent commits and 2,000+ stars
cons: Requires self-hosting (e.g. via Docker) and technical setup to run
No first-party managed-cloud pricing page on cortezaproject.org; hosted instances are offered by third parties such as Planet Crust
Smaller community and ecosystem than major commercial CRMs like Salesforce

faqs.question: Is Corteza free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Corteza is fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to download and self-host.

faqs.question: What license does Corteza use?
faqs.answer: Corteza is released under the Apache License 2.0.

faqs.question: Can I run Corteza without managing my own server?
faqs.answer: Corteza is designed to be self-hosted via Docker; hosted cloud instances are also offered by third parties such as Planet Crust, separate from the core open-source project.

faqs.question: Is Corteza only a CRM?
faqs.answer: No. Corteza is a broader low-code platform that includes a CRM module alongside a unified workspace and enterprise messaging.

use_cases.title: Open-source CRM without vendor lock-in
use_cases.description: Organizations that want a self-hosted alternative to closed-source CRMs and full control of their data.
use_cases.audience: IT teams and organizations avoiding vendor lock-in

use_cases.title: Custom low-code business apps
use_cases.description: Building bespoke records-based workflow apps on top of the same platform that powers the CRM.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Corteza CRM?
paragraphs: Corteza is an open-source, low-code platform that includes a CRM module for managing leads and accounts, alongside a unified workspace and enterprise messaging. It is released under the Apache 2.0 license, and its code has been contributed to the Commons Conservancy Software Foundation.
The platform is self-hosted, typically via Docker, and includes a flattened role-based access control (RBAC) system so organizations can apply their own internal security policies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Corteza CRM is for
paragraphs: Corteza suits organizations that want an open-source, self-hosted CRM and are comfortable running and maintaining their own infrastructure, as well as developers who want to extend the same low-code platform to build other records-based business apps.

pros: Fully open source with code available on GitHub, so any feature can be modified
No vendor lock-in — can be self-hosted on your own infrastructure at no cost
Built on a modern, well-documented stack (React, TypeScript, Shadcn UI, Supabase)
Includes SSO and an MCP Server for AI-assistant integration out of the box

Self-hosting requires managing your own Supabase/PostgreSQL infrastructure

faqs.question: Is Atomic CRM free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be self-hosted for free since it is fully open source; Marmelab also offers paid managed plans starting at €25/month.

faqs.question: Who built Atomic CRM?
faqs.answer: Atomic CRM is built by Marmelab, using React, TypeScript, Shadcn UI, Tailwind CSS, and Supabase.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Atomic CRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting is supported for full data sovereignty, in addition to Marmelab's Supabase-managed hosting option.

faqs.question: Does Atomic CRM support single sign-on?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports SSO via Google, Azure, Keycloak, and Auth0.

use_cases.title: Customizable CRM without vendor lock-in
use_cases.description: Teams that want a CRM they can fully modify at the code level rather than adapting to a closed platform.

use_cases.title: Managed hosted CRM
use_cases.description: Teams that want the open-source toolkit without operating their own infrastructure, using Marmelab's Starter or Team plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Atomic CRM?
paragraphs: Atomic CRM is an open-source CRM toolkit from Marmelab, built with React, TypeScript, Shadcn UI, Tailwind CSS, and Supabase. It covers contact management, a Kanban-based sales pipeline, task reminders, and activity tracking.

Because the full source is available on GitHub, teams can modify any feature to fit their process instead of working around a closed vendor's roadmap. Marmelab also offers Supabase-managed hosting as a paid alternative to self-hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Atomic CRM is for
paragraphs: Atomic CRM fits teams that want to self-host a CRM and customize it at the code level, as well as smaller sales teams that prefer a managed hosted plan without giving up the option to self-host later.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Krayin CRM?
paragraphs: Krayin CRM is an open-source, self-hosted CRM built on Laravel and maintained by Webkul. It covers sales pipeline management, marketing automation, and multi-channel customer support (including VoIP and live chat) in a single self-hosted application.
The project has more than 23,000 GitHub stars and is actively developed, with extensions available for VoIP, multi-tenant SaaS deployments, and Google Workspace synchronization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Krayin CRM is for
paragraphs: Krayin suits businesses that want a free, self-hosted CRM built on a familiar Laravel/PHP stack, and that are comfortable managing their own server or contracting managed hosting through Webkul or a third party.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Frappe Books?
paragraphs: Frappe Books is a free, open-source desktop accounting application for small and medium businesses. It supports double-entry bookkeeping, invoicing, a point-of-sale module, and standard reports like Profit and Loss and Balance Sheet.
Built with Electron, it runs offline on Windows, macOS, and Linux, storing all data in a local SQLite file so users retain full ownership of their financial data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Frappe Books is for
paragraphs: Frappe Books fits freelancers and small business owners who want free, offline accounting software and prefer keeping their financial data on their own machine rather than in a third-party cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Akaunting is for

Paid plans add multi-currency support, inventory tracking, budgeting, cost-center analysis, and multi-branch support as you move up tiers, with a 14-day free trial available.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Bigcapital is for
paragraphs: Bigcapital fits businesses that want an open-source, self-hostable alternative to accounting platforms like QuickBooks or Xero, as well as those that would rather use Bigcapital's own hosted cloud plans.

It is licensed under AGPLv3, includes a REST JSON API covering nearly every part of the app, and never contacts external servers unless explicitly configured to do so.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Firefly III is for
paragraphs: Firefly III fits individuals who want full control and privacy over their personal finance data and are comfortable self-hosting via Docker or a similar deployment method, including developers who want to automate finance tracking through its API.

Active open-source community with crowdsourced translations via Weblate
cons: Multi-device sync requires self-hosting a sync server, which takes technical setup

faqs.question: Is Actual Budget free?

faqs.question: Can I self-host Actual Budget?

faqs.question: Does Actual Budget support bank syncing?

faqs.question: Can I migrate from YNAB?

faqs.question: What platforms does Actual Budget run on?
faqs.answer: It's available as downloadable apps for Windows, Mac, and Linux, plus deployable via Docker.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Actual Budget?
paragraphs: Actual Budget is a free, open-source personal finance application built around envelope budgeting, where users assign every dollar of income to a category.
It uses a local-first architecture, meaning the app keeps working even without a network connection, and data can be synced across devices through a self-hosted sync server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Actual Budget is for
paragraphs: Actual Budget suits privacy-focused users and self-hosters who want a free, open-source alternative to subscription budgeting apps like YNAB, and are comfortable running or renting a small sync server.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Lunch Money?
paragraphs: Lunch Money is a paid, web-first personal budgeting and net-worth tracking app founded in 2019 and based in Toronto, Canada.
It syncs bank transactions via Plaid, supports multiple currencies and cryptocurrency accounts, and includes a rules engine for automatic categorization, with all features available on every plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Lunch Money is for

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Ghostfolio?
paragraphs: Ghostfolio is an open-source wealth management application, first published in 2021, for tracking investment transactions, portfolio performance, and risk.
It can be self-hosted for free under the AGPLv3 license via Docker, or used through Ghostfolio Premium, a managed cloud offering that bundles professional market data providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Ghostfolio is for
paragraphs: Ghostfolio suits individual investors who want a transparent, open-source way to track portfolio performance and risk, whether they prefer self-hosting their own instance or paying for the managed Premium cloud service.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Planka?
paragraphs: Planka is a modern, Trello-style Kanban board application for organizing and visualizing team tasks. It is open source, with a free self-hosted Community edition and a commercial Pro tier that adds branding, guest roles, and additional views.
The project is maintained by PLANKA Software GmbH (Kempten, Germany) and can be deployed via Docker on your own infrastructure, or run as a managed cloud instance through PLANKA's hosted plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Planka is for
paragraphs: Planka suits teams that want a self-hosted alternative to Trello-style boards, from small teams using the free Community edition to agencies and enterprises that need custom branding, guest access, or cross-company usage rights via the Pro tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Redmine?
paragraphs: Redmine is a flexible, free and open-source project management web application written in Ruby on Rails. It is cross-platform and cross-database, and has been developed since 2006.
It combines issue tracking with Gantt charts, calendars, wikis, forums, time tracking, and integration with source control systems like Git and SVN.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Redmine is for
paragraphs: Redmine fits software teams and organizations that want a mature, no-cost, self-hosted project and issue tracker with deep customization through plugins and custom fields, and are willing to manage their own server infrastructure.

faqs.question: What is Phorge's relationship to Phabricator?
faqs.answer: Phorge is a community-maintained fork of Phabricator, the developer toolsuite originally built at Facebook, which became unmaintained in mid-2021.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Phorge?
paragraphs: Phorge is a free, open-source, community-maintained fork of Phabricator, the developer toolsuite originally built at Facebook that became unmaintained in mid-2021. Phorge's community fork effort began in 2021, with an initial public release in September 2022.
It provides code review, repository hosting, task tracking, wikis, and rule-based automation (Herald) in a single self-hosted platform, released under the Apache 2.0 license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Phorge is for
paragraphs: Phorge is for engineering teams that relied on Phabricator and need an actively maintained, self-hosted continuation, and for organizations that want integrated code review and task tracking under their own infrastructure. The Wikimedia Foundation is a notable production adopter.

faqs.question: Can I sync tasks across multiple devices?

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Taskwarrior?

It is developed by Gothenburg Bit Factory and has an active ecosystem that includes companion tools such as Timewarrior for time tracking and Taskshell.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Taskwarrior is for
paragraphs: Taskwarrior suits developers, sysadmins, and other command-line-comfortable users who want fast, scriptable task management with deep customization through filters, UDAs, and hooks, rather than a graphical to-do app.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Super Productivity?

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Super Productivity is for

faqs.question: What does the paid tier include?
faqs.answer: The PRO tier ($64 one-time) adds third-party S3/WebDAV sync and backup. The subscription tier ($148 one-time as listed) adds official cloud sync, 8GB of official cloud storage, cloud asset hosting, and a cloud inbox.

faqs.question: Can I self-host SiYuan?

Enterprise compliance focus may add complexity for teams that just need basic team chat
Federation and advanced Apps-Engine features may require technical setup

faqs.question: Is Rocket.Chat open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Rocket.Chat?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports self-hosted deployment via Docker or Kubernetes, including air-gapped environments.

faqs.question: How much does Rocket.Chat cost?
faqs.answer: Paid Commercial, Government, and Defense plans use custom quotes; you need to contact sales for pricing.

faqs.question: When was Rocket.Chat founded?
faqs.answer: It started as a side project of four friends in 2015.

faqs.question: What compliance certifications does Rocket.Chat have?
faqs.answer: SOC 2, ISO/IEC 27001, GDPR, HIPAA, and DoD Authorization to Operate up to IL6.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted secure team chat

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Rocket.Chat?
paragraphs: Rocket.Chat is an open-source communications platform combining messaging, voice, video, and AI-powered automation, founded in 2015 as a side project of four friends.
It supports self-hosted, cloud, and air-gapped deployments, and holds certifications including SOC 2, ISO/IEC 27001, GDPR, HIPAA, and DoD Authorization to Operate up to IL6.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Rocket.Chat is for
paragraphs: Rocket.Chat is used by security-conscious organizations that want a self-hosted, open-source chat platform, and by government and defense agencies that need sovereign or air-gapped communication.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source team chat app organized around topic-based threads for asynchronous team communication.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Zulip is an open-source team chat tool with topic threading. Free self-hosting or Zulip Cloud plans from $6.67/user/month.

features.title: Topic-based threading
features.description: Conversations are organized into topics within channels, keeping discussions structured and easy to catch up on asynchronously.

features.title: Unified inbox
features.description: An inbox view surfaces unread messages across channels and topics so users can track what needs attention.

features.title: Self-hosting or cloud
features.description: Zulip can be self-hosted for full data control or run as a hosted Zulip Cloud service, with migration possible between the two.

features.title: Integrations and API
features.description: Zulip offers hundreds of integrations and a REST API for connecting to other tools and building custom workflows.

features.title: Guest accounts
features.description: Organizations can invite external collaborators as guest users with restricted access.

features.title: Enterprise authentication
features.description: Paid plans add SAML SSO (e.g., Okta, OneLogin), SCIM user sync, and custom domains.

pros: 100% open-source with a free self-hosted option
Topic threading keeps large teams' conversations organized better than flat channels
Free or discounted plans for open-source projects, nonprofits, and academic research
Both cloud-hosted and self-hosted deployment options available
Desktop apps for Mac, Windows, and Linux plus iOS and Android mobile apps
cons: Plus and Business plans require a minimum number of users (10 and 25 respectively)
Free Cloud plan limits search history to 10,000 messages
Enterprise-grade SSO (SAML, SCIM) is gated behind paid tiers
Topic-based model has a learning curve for teams used to flat channel chat

faqs.question: Is Zulip free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Zulip offers a free Cloud plan and a free self-hosted plan with all core features; paid plans add unlimited search history, higher storage, and enterprise authentication.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Zulip?
faqs.answer: Yes, Zulip is open source under the Apache 2 license and can be self-hosted, with paid self-hosted tiers available for organizations that want more storage or support.

faqs.question: Does Zulip offer discounts for nonprofits or open-source projects?
faqs.answer: Yes, qualifying open-source projects, academic research groups, nonprofits, and small organizations can receive free or discounted access.

faqs.question: What platforms does Zulip support?
faqs.answer: Zulip has a web app, desktop apps for Mac, Windows, and Linux, and mobile apps for iOS and Android.

faqs.question: Who maintains Zulip?
faqs.answer: Zulip was originally built by Zulip, Inc., open-sourced by Dropbox in 2015, and is now stewarded by Kandra Labs, which is owned by the nonprofit Zulip Foundation.

use_cases.title: Distributed team communication
use_cases.description: Teams spread across time zones use Zulip's topic threading to hold organized, asynchronous discussions without losing context.

use_cases.title: Open-source project coordination
use_cases.description: Open-source communities use Zulip's free tier and threaded channels to coordinate contributors and discussions.

use_cases.title: Data-sovereign deployments
use_cases.description: Organizations that need to keep chat data on their own infrastructure self-host Zulip instead of using the cloud service.

features: Unlimited mobile notifications for eligible communities

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Zulip?
paragraphs: Zulip is an open-source team chat application built around topic-based threading, which groups messages within channels into distinct topics so conversations stay organized and searchable.

It was originally built as a startup, acquired by Dropbox in 2014, and open-sourced under the Apache 2 license in 2015. Development is now led by Kandra Labs, which is owned by the nonprofit Zulip Foundation.
Zulip is available as a hosted Zulip Cloud service or as self-hosted software, with the ability to migrate between the two.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Zulip is for
paragraphs: Zulip is aimed at distributed teams, open-source projects, academic and research communities, and organizations that want asynchronous, threaded discussions rather than a flat, real-time chat feed.

enrichment.short_description: Secure messaging and collaboration app built on the open Matrix protocol, offering end-to-end encrypted chat and calls.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Element is an end-to-end encrypted messaging app built on the Matrix protocol, with a free open-source Community edition and paid Enterprise/Sovereign tiers.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: Messages and calls are end-to-end encrypted by default across Element clients.

features.title: Decentralized/federated architecture
features.description: Built on the Matrix open standard, Element supports federation and interoperability between separate organizations' servers.

features.title: Element Call
features.description: Built-in voice and video calling is included as part of the Element client.

features.title: Air-gapped deployment
features.description: The Sovereign plan supports deployments that operate entirely without internet connectivity.

features.title: Enterprise identity management
features.description: Enterprise and Sovereign plans add LDAP, Active Directory, and OIDC/SSO integration, plus auditing and retention policies.

features.title: Self-hosted homeserver
features.description: Organizations can run their own Matrix homeserver (Synapse or Synapse Pro via Element Server Suite Pro) instead of relying on Element's infrastructure.

pros: Open-source Community edition available for free, self-hosted use
End-to-end encryption included across plans
Decentralized Matrix protocol avoids vendor lock-in and single points of failure
Used by government and defense organizations, indicating strong security vetting
Holds Cyber Essentials Plus, ISO/IEC 27001:2022, and OpenChain ISO/IEC 5230 certifications
cons: Enterprise and Sovereign plan pricing is not published and requires contacting sales
Full enterprise identity/security features require the paid Enterprise Server Suite Pro
Federated architecture adds operational complexity compared to a single hosted SaaS chat tool

faqs.question: Is Element free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Element's Community edition is free and open source under AGPL for self-hosted, non-professional deployments and evaluations.

faqs.question: What protocol does Element use?
faqs.answer: Element is built on Matrix, an open, decentralized communication standard.

faqs.question: Does Element support end-to-end encryption?
faqs.answer: Yes, end-to-end encryption is included on all plans for messages and calls.

faqs.question: Can Element run without internet access?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Sovereign plan supports air-gapped deployments via Element Server Suite Pro.

faqs.question: Who uses Element?
faqs.answer: Element is used by government and defense organizations, including bodies referenced by Element such as NATO, US Space Force, and the Bundeswehr, as well as private enterprises.

use_cases.title: Secure government communication
use_cases.description: Government and defense organizations use Element for encrypted, sovereign-controlled messaging that can run air-gapped.

use_cases.title: Cross-organization collaboration
use_cases.description: Matrix's federated architecture lets separate organizations communicate securely without merging infrastructure.
use_cases.audience: Enterprises needing cross-border or cross-org messaging

use_cases.title: Self-hosted team chat
use_cases.description: Teams that want full control of their data run the free Community edition on their own servers.

features: Element Server Suite Pro with Synapse Pro
Element Server Suite Pro with Synapse Pro

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Element?
paragraphs: Element is a secure messaging and collaboration platform built on the Matrix open standard, providing end-to-end encrypted chat and calls with a decentralized, federated architecture.

The company behind Element was founded in 2017 as New Vector Ltd, a spin-out from Amdocs, and rebranded to Element in 2020. It is headquartered in the United Kingdom with additional entities in Germany, France, and the United States.

Element offers a free, open-source Community edition alongside paid Enterprise and Sovereign tiers built around Element Server Suite Pro for organizations needing advanced identity management, support SLAs, or air-gapped deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Element is for
paragraphs: Element is aimed at organizations that need sovereign, encrypted communication — including government, defense, and enterprises that require data control, federation between organizations, or air-gapped deployment.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source WebRTC video conferencing platform with peer-to-peer, SFU, and end-to-end encrypted call modes.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MiroTalk is an open-source WebRTC video conferencing platform you can self-host, offering P2P, SFU, and encrypted call variants.

features.title: Peer-to-peer video calls (P2P)
features.description: MiroTalk P2P connects participants directly over WebRTC without routing media through a central server.

features.title: SFU mode for larger meetings
features.description: MiroTalk SFU uses a selective forwarding unit architecture to support more participants than pure P2P allows.

features.title: High-resolution video
features.description: Supports video up to 8K resolution at 60fps along with screen sharing and recording.

features.title: Chat and collaborative whiteboard
features.description: Includes text chat with markdown support and a collaborative whiteboard alongside video.

features.title: REST API and embedding
features.description: Offers a full REST API and embeddable widgets/iframe integration for adding video calling to other sites.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Can be deployed on your own server via Docker or Node.js in minutes.

pros: Free, open-source, and self-hostable under AGPLv3
No download, plug-in, or login required to join a call
Multiple architecture variants (P2P, SFU, C2C) to fit different use cases
Full REST API for embedding video calling into other products
Supports up to 8K/60fps video and multi-language interfaces
cons: The root mirotalk.com domain does not resolve to an active marketing site, which can make discovery confusing
Peer-to-peer mode's quality depends on client hardware and network conditions rather than server infrastructure
AGPLv3 requires derivative works to also be open source unless a separate commercial license is purchased
No official hosted enterprise support plan is documented

faqs.question: Is MiroTalk free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, MiroTalk is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license, and can be self-hosted at no licensing cost.

faqs.question: Do I need to create an account to join a MiroTalk call?
faqs.answer: No, MiroTalk is designed so users can join calls without downloads, plug-ins, or logins.

faqs.question: What's the difference between MiroTalk P2P and SFU?
faqs.answer: MiroTalk P2P connects participants directly to each other, while MiroTalk SFU routes media through a selective forwarding unit server to support larger meetings.

faqs.question: Can I self-host MiroTalk?
faqs.answer: Yes, MiroTalk can be deployed on your own server using Docker or Node.js.

faqs.question: Is there a commercial license for MiroTalk?
faqs.answer: Yes, in addition to the free AGPLv3 license, commercial licenses are available via CodeCanyon for closed-source use.

use_cases.title: Embedded video calling
use_cases.description: Developers use MiroTalk's REST API and iframe embedding to add video calls directly into their own websites or apps.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused self-hosted meetings
use_cases.description: Organizations self-host MiroTalk to keep video call infrastructure entirely under their own control.

use_cases.title: Ad-hoc peer-to-peer calls
use_cases.description: Individuals use MiroTalk P2P for quick, no-signup video calls shared via a link.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is MiroTalk?
paragraphs: MiroTalk is an open-source WebRTC video conferencing platform that can be self-hosted on your own server. It is distributed in several variants, including MiroTalk P2P for direct peer-to-peer calls, MiroTalk SFU for larger meetings routed through a selective forwarding unit, and MiroTalk C2C for end-to-end encrypted cam-to-cam calls.
The project is developed by Miroslav Pejic and is licensed under AGPLv3, with a commercial license available through CodeCanyon for use cases requiring closed-source distribution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who MiroTalk is for
paragraphs: MiroTalk is aimed at developers who want to embed video calling into their own products, and at organizations that want a self-hosted, no-login video conferencing tool.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source virtual classroom platform for online education with whiteboards, breakout rooms, and LMS integrations.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BigBlueButton is an open-source virtual classroom platform with whiteboards, breakout rooms, and polling, self-hostable or commercially hosted.

features.title: Breakout rooms

features.title: Polling and hand-raise tools

features.title: Session recording and playback
features.description: Sessions can be recorded and played back in multiple resolutions afterward.

features.title: LMS integrations
features.description: Integrates with learning management systems including Canvas, Moodle, Sakai, Jenzabar, D2L, and Schoology.

pros: Open source under LGPL-3.0 with a self-hosted install option
Purpose-built for online education rather than general business meetings
Deep integration with major LMS platforms
Used daily across 65+ languages according to the project
Installable in about 30 minutes via the bbb-install.sh script on Ubuntu
cons: Self-hosting requires server administration and Ubuntu 22.04 infrastructure
Focused specifically on classroom/education use cases rather than general business video conferencing
Commercial hosted pricing is not published on the main site and requires going through bigbluebutton.com

faqs.question: Is BigBlueButton free?
faqs.answer: Yes, BigBlueButton is open source under the LGPL-3.0 license and can be self-hosted for free; commercial hosting is also available.

faqs.question: Can I self-host BigBlueButton?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be installed on Ubuntu 22.04 using the bbb-install.sh script, with full documentation at docs.bigbluebutton.org.

faqs.question: Does BigBlueButton integrate with learning management systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with LMS platforms including Canvas, Moodle Cloud, Sakai, Jenzabar, D2L, and Schoology.

faqs.question: Who created BigBlueButton?
faqs.answer: BigBlueButton originated from Carleton University's Technology Innovation Management program in Ottawa, Canada in 2007, and is now sponsored by Blindside Networks.

faqs.question: Does BigBlueButton support breakout rooms and whiteboards?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes breakout rooms, a multi-user whiteboard, shared notes, and screen sharing.

use_cases.title: K-12 online classrooms
use_cases.description: Schools use BigBlueButton's classroom-specific tools like polling and breakout rooms for remote instruction.

use_cases.title: Higher education distance learning
use_cases.description: Universities integrate BigBlueButton with LMS platforms like Canvas and Moodle for online courses.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted institutional deployment
use_cases.description: Institutions with data control requirements self-host BigBlueButton on their own servers rather than using a commercial host.
use_cases.audience: IT departments at educational and government institutions

long_form_blocks.heading: What is BigBlueButton?
paragraphs: BigBlueButton is an open-source virtual classroom platform purpose-built for online education, distinguishing it from general-purpose video conferencing tools. It includes a multi-user whiteboard, breakout rooms, document sharing, polling, and session recording.

The project originated from Carleton University's Technology Innovation Management program in Ottawa, Canada, in 2007, and is now sponsored by Blindside Networks, which offers commercial hosting and support.
It is licensed under LGPL-3.0 and can be self-hosted on Ubuntu 22.04, or used through commercial hosted services.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who BigBlueButton is for
paragraphs: BigBlueButton is built for K-12 schools, higher education institutions, and government organizations delivering online instruction, particularly those already using an LMS.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight, self-hosted MIT-licensed videoconference server built for low server resource requirements.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Galene is a free, open-source videoconference server built in Go that is easy to self-host and runs on modest server resources.

features.title: Low resource requirements
features.description: Galene is designed to be easy to host and requires very moderate server resources compared to other conferencing servers.

features.title: Multiple codec support
features.description: Supports VP8, VP9, H.264, and AV1 video codecs along with simulcast and scalable video coding.

features.title: Screen sharing and background blur
features.description: Client features include screen sharing, background blur, and file transfer.

features.title: Recording and text chat

features.title: Group moderation tools
features.description: Includes moderation controls for managing participants in group calls.

features.title: Portable across platforms
features.description: The server runs on Go using the Pion WebRTC implementation and is tested on Linux/amd64, Linux/arm64, macOS, and Windows.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
Requires very moderate server resources, making it cheap to self-host
Supports modern codecs including AV1 and VP9 with simulcast
Portable across Linux, macOS, and Windows
In active academic use at institutions like Université de Paris and Sorbonne Université
cons: Does not perform end-to-end encryption, so self-hosting is recommended for privacy
Requires manual setup or Yunohost installation rather than a managed hosted option
No official company or commercial support channel; maintained by an individual author
Mobile support is limited to mobile browsers rather than native apps

faqs.question: Is Galene free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Galene is free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Does Galene support end-to-end encryption?
faqs.answer: No, Galene does not perform end-to-end encryption, so the author recommends self-hosting for optimal privacy.

faqs.question: What platforms can Galene run on?
faqs.answer: The server is tested on Linux/amd64 and Linux/arm64, and also runs on macOS and Windows.

faqs.question: Who maintains Galene?
faqs.answer: Galene is authored by Juliusz Chroboczek, with development currently supported by NLnet's NGI0Core program.

faqs.question: How do I install Galene?
faqs.answer: Galene can be installed manually or via Yunohost.

use_cases.title: University lectures and seminars
use_cases.description: Universities such as Université de Paris and Sorbonne Université use Galene for lectures, practicals, and seminars.

use_cases.title: Low-resource self-hosted meetings
use_cases.description: Organizations with limited server capacity self-host Galene because of its moderate resource requirements.
use_cases.audience: Small organizations and individuals hosting their own conferencing infrastructure

use_cases.title: Conference and event video
use_cases.description: Galene has been used to run video for events and conferences such as SOCS'2020 and LibrePlanet 2024.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Galene?
paragraphs: Galene is a videoconference server designed to be easy to host and to require very moderate server resources. It was originally built for academic settings but has proven suitable for traditional meetings as well.

The server is written in Go using the Pion WebRTC implementation, supports codecs including VP8, VP9, H.264, and AV1 with simulcast and scalable video coding, and is free and open source under the MIT license.
It is authored by Juliusz Chroboczek, with development supported by NLnet's NGI0Core program.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Galene is for
paragraphs: Galene is aimed at universities, privacy-conscious organizations, and individuals who want to self-host a lightweight video conferencing server without heavy infrastructure requirements.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, peer-to-peer video group chat that runs in the browser with no installation required.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Briefing is a free, open-source, peer-to-peer video group chat that works in the browser, with self-hosting and iOS app options.

features.title: Peer-to-peer video chat
features.description: Video and data are sent directly between participants (mesh architecture) rather than routed through a central server under normal operation.

features.title: Desktop sharing
features.description: Participants can share their screen during a call.

features.title: Text chat
features.description: Includes in-call text chat alongside video.

features.title: Simple invitation links
features.description: Rooms can be started and shared instantly via a link at brie.fi/ng.

features.title: iOS app
features.description: A free iOS app is available in addition to the browser-based web client.

features.title: Iframe embedding
features.description: Briefing can be embedded into other websites via iframe.

pros: Free to use instantly at brie.fi/ng with no account required
Open source under AGPL-3.0
Peer-to-peer architecture means call data doesn't normally pass through the server
Self-hostable via Docker on providers like fly.io, Render, Google Cloud, AWS, or Azure
Actively maintained, with releases continuing into the current release cycle
cons: A commercial license is required for white-labeling or closed-source derivatives
Peer-to-peer mesh architecture can strain performance in larger group calls
No Android app is documented, only iOS
Maintained primarily by a single developer rather than a larger organization

faqs.question: Is Briefing free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Briefing is free to use at brie.fi/ng and is open source under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Briefing?
faqs.answer: Yes, Briefing can be self-hosted via Docker on services such as fly.io, Render, Google Cloud, AWS, or Azure.

faqs.question: Does Briefing require an account or app install?
faqs.answer: No, Briefing runs directly in the browser with no download required; a free iOS app is also available.

faqs.question: Is Briefing's data routed through a server?
faqs.answer: No, Briefing uses a peer-to-peer mesh architecture, so call data does not normally travel over the server.

faqs.question: Is there a commercial license for Briefing?
faqs.answer: Yes, a commercial license is available for white-labeling or closed-source use, separate from the free AGPL-3.0 license.

use_cases.title: Quick ad-hoc video calls
use_cases.description: Users start an instant, link-based video call at brie.fi/ng without creating an account.
use_cases.audience: Individuals needing a fast, no-signup video call

use_cases.title: Embedded video chat
use_cases.description: Website owners embed Briefing via iframe to add video chat directly into their own site.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted private video chat
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious users deploy their own Briefing instance via Docker for full control over their video chat infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Briefing?
paragraphs: Briefing is an open-source, peer-to-peer video group chat application that runs directly in the browser using WebRTC, with no software installation required. Video and data are sent directly between participants under normal operation rather than routed through a central server.

It is available for free at brie.fi/ng, can be embedded via iframe, and can be self-hosted using Docker on providers such as fly.io, Render, Google Cloud, AWS, or Azure. The project is licensed under AGPL-3.0, with a separate commercial license available for white-labeling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Briefing is for
paragraphs: Briefing is aimed at individuals who want an instant, no-signup video call, and at developers who want to embed or self-host a privacy-focused, peer-to-peer video chat tool.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source video meetings, chat, and webinars app integrated into the Nextcloud collaboration platform.

features.title: AI-powered summaries
features.description: Offers AI-powered chat and call summaries and live meeting translation.

features.title: Screen sharing and webinars
features.description: Supports screen sharing, webinars, and public video conferencing.

features.title: Deep Nextcloud integration

features.title: Cross-platform bridging

pros: Fully self-hosted and open source, supporting air-gapped deployments
Deep integration with the broader Nextcloud suite (Files, Groupware, Office)
Supports on-premises deployment for GDPR compliance and data sovereignty
Desktop apps for Windows, macOS, and Linux plus mobile apps for Android and iOS
Used by organizations such as the Austrian Federal Ministry, Deutsche Telekom, and Amnesty International

faqs.question: Is Nextcloud Talk free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nextcloud Talk is open source and free to self-host as part of Nextcloud; paid Enterprise Talk plans add extended support and scaling features.

faqs.question: Does Nextcloud Talk support end-to-end encryption?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports end-to-end encrypted calls and federated chat/video.

faqs.question: What platforms does Nextcloud Talk support?
faqs.answer: It's available on the web, desktop apps for Windows, macOS, and Linux, and mobile apps for Android and iOS.

faqs.question: Can Nextcloud Talk run without internet access?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports on-premises and air-gapped deployments.

faqs.question: What does the Enterprise Talk plan cost?
faqs.answer: Published Enterprise Talk pricing starts at €42/user/year for 100 users, dropping to €31.50/user/year at 250 users.

use_cases.title: Data-sovereign video meetings
use_cases.description: Government agencies and enterprises self-host Nextcloud Talk to keep meeting data within their own infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Integrated team collaboration

use_cases.title: Air-gapped communication
use_cases.description: Organizations with strict network isolation requirements run Nextcloud Talk without internet connectivity.

features: Secure online meetings, chat, and web conferencing
Secure online meetings, chat, and web conferencing

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Nextcloud Talk?
paragraphs: Nextcloud Talk is a self-hosted, open-source communication app for meetings, chat, and webinars, integrated into the Nextcloud collaboration platform. It supports end-to-end encrypted calls, federated chat and video, threaded conversations, and AI-powered call summaries.

It integrates with other Nextcloud apps such as Files, Groupware, and Office, and can bridge with external networks like Teams, Slack, and Matrix. Nextcloud offers paid Enterprise Talk plans with extended support and a High Performance Backend for scaling large calls.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Nextcloud Talk is for
paragraphs: Nextcloud Talk is aimed at government agencies, universities, enterprises, and service providers that need self-hosted, GDPR-compliant video meetings and chat integrated with broader file and office collaboration tools.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source Matrix homeserver implementation written in Python/Twisted and Rust, maintained by Element.

features.title: Single Sign-On support
features.description: Supports SSO authentication for user login.

features.title: Admin API
features.description: Provides an administrative API for managing users and the instance.

features.title: Pluggable modules
features.description: Supports pluggable modules for extending server behavior.

features.title: Worker processes for scaling
features.description: Can be split into worker processes to scale larger deployments.

features.title: Monitoring and metrics
features.description: Integrates with monitoring and metrics tooling, plus structured logging options.

pros: Free and open source, available under AGPL-3.0
Reference implementation of the open Matrix protocol, ensuring broad interoperability
Actively maintained by Element on GitHub with an open contribution process
Supports horizontal scaling via worker processes for larger deployments
A commercial license (Element Commercial License) is available for organizations that don't want AGPL obligations
cons: Requires self-hosting and server administration; there is no first-party managed Synapse cloud

faqs.answer: Synapse is written primarily in Python/Twisted, with some components in Rust.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Synapse?
paragraphs: Synapse is the reference server implementation of the Matrix protocol, a decentralized standard for secure communication. It is written primarily in Python/Twisted with some Rust components and is maintained by Element on GitHub.

Synapse is free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license, with a separate commercial license available from Element for organizations that need different terms. It supports SSO, an admin API, pluggable modules, and worker processes for scaling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Synapse is for
paragraphs: Synapse is aimed at operators who want to run their own Matrix homeserver, and developers building on the Matrix protocol who need a reference server implementation.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Mastodon?
paragraphs: Mastodon is free, open-source microblogging software launched in 2016 by Eugen Rochko. Instead of one central company, Mastodon runs as thousands of independently operated servers that interconnect via the ActivityPub protocol, collectively known as the fediverse.
Users can join an existing public server or self-host their own, with each server setting its own moderation rules while still being able to follow and interact with accounts on other servers.

paragraphs: Mastodon suits individuals who want an ad-free, non-algorithmic social feed, as well as communities and organizations that want to self-host and moderate their own corner of the social web.

Modular add-ons (POS, scanning, kiosk) for on-site event operations

faqs.question: Is pretix free?
faqs.answer: The self-hosted core software is free and open source under AGPLv3. The hosted SaaS charges 2.5% per paid ticket (capped at €15) plus payment provider fees, with a limited free tier for free tickets.

faqs.question: Where is pretix based?
faqs.answer: pretix GmbH is headquartered in Heidelberg, Germany.

faqs.question: Can pretix be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source core (AGPLv3) can be self-hosted with the same feature set as the hosted service.

faqs.question: How does pretix charge for free tickets?
faqs.answer: The first 500 free tickets issued per year on the hosted SaaS are free; additional free tickets cost €0.50 each.

use_cases.title: Conference and festival ticketing
use_cases.description: Sell and manage tickets for conferences, festivals, and concerts.

use_cases.title: On-site check-in and access control
use_cases.description: Use pretixSCAN and pretixPOS for badge printing, entry control, and on-site sales.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Pretix?
paragraphs: Pretix is open-source ticketing software for conferences, festivals, exhibitions, museums, and other events, developed since 2013 by pretix GmbH in Heidelberg, Germany.

The core software is free and self-hostable under the AGPLv3 license. Organizers who prefer not to self-host can use pretix's hosted SaaS, which charges no monthly fee but takes a percentage of paid ticket sales plus payment provider fees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Pretix is for
paragraphs: Pretix suits event organizers who need online ticket sales alongside on-site tools like point-of-sale, access control, and badge printing, whether they prefer to self-host or use the hosted service.

pros: Free, unlimited-seat self-hosted Community Edition
Can connect directly to an existing Postgres or MySQL database instead of requiring data migration
Large community per vendor claims: 64k+ GitHub stars and 20M+ Docker downloads
Auto-generated REST APIs and a built-in App Store of integrations
Both cloud-hosted and self-hosted deployment options, with a genuine free cloud tier

faqs.question: Is NocoDB free?
faqs.answer: Yes. NocoDB offers a free cloud plan (3 editor seats, 1,000 records) and a free, self-hosted Community Edition.

faqs.question: What license does NocoDB use?
faqs.answer: The self-hosted Community Edition is distributed under the Sustainable Use License, a source-available license, after the project moved away from AGPLv3.

faqs.question: Can NocoDB connect to an existing database?
faqs.answer: Yes, NocoDB can turn an existing Postgres or MySQL database into a spreadsheet-style interface rather than requiring data import.

faqs.question: Does NocoDB provide an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, every base gets auto-generated REST APIs, webhooks, and an SDK.

faqs.question: Is NocoDB available self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, via Docker, binary installers for macOS/Linux/Windows, or an install script.

use_cases.title: Internal business databases
use_cases.description: Teams replace spreadsheets with a shared, permissioned database for tracking projects, inventory, or CRM-style records.

use_cases.title: UI layer over an existing SQL database
use_cases.description: Developers connect NocoDB to a Postgres/MySQL database to give non-technical staff a spreadsheet interface without migrating data.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted data platform
use_cases.description: Organizations that need to keep data on their own infrastructure deploy the free Community Edition via Docker.
use_cases.audience: IT and compliance teams needing data residency control

long_form_blocks.heading: What is NocoDB?
paragraphs: NocoDB is a no-code database platform that presents data through a familiar spreadsheet-style interface while storing it in a real relational database. It can either use its own database or connect directly to an existing Postgres or MySQL database, turning that data into views (Grid, Gallery, Form, Kanban, Calendar) that teams can filter, sort, and edit.

It is available as a hosted cloud product with tiered pricing, and as a self-hosted Community Edition that can be deployed with Docker or binary installers. The self-hosted edition is distributed under the Sustainable Use License, a source-available license the project adopted after moving away from AGPLv3.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who NocoDB is for
paragraphs: NocoDB suits teams and developers who want a spreadsheet-like interface over structured data without giving up SQL underneath, especially those who already have a Postgres or MySQL database and want a collaborative UI on top of it, or who want to self-host their database tooling.

enrichment.short_description: Baserow is an open-source, self-hostable no-code database and application builder positioned as an Airtable alternative.

Cloud version is GDPR, HIPAA, and SOC 2 Type II compliant
65+ templates included on the free plan
Multiple self-hosting paths, including one-click deploys on Cloudron, Railway, Elestio, and Heroku

faqs.question: Is Baserow open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Baserow's core is released under the MIT license; premium and enterprise features are offered as a source-available add-on under an open-core model.

faqs.question: Can Baserow be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, via Docker, Docker Compose, Helm, or hosted providers like Cloudron, Railway, Elestio, Heroku, Render, DigitalOcean, and AWS.

faqs.question: Does Baserow have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Free plan includes 3,000 rows per workspace, 2GB storage, and 65+ templates.

faqs.question: Is Baserow HIPAA and SOC 2 compliant?
faqs.answer: According to Baserow's website, the cloud-hosted version is GDPR, HIPAA, and SOC 2 Type II compliant.

faqs.question: Does Baserow include AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, its Kuma AI assistant can generate data structures and automations from natural-language descriptions, starting on the Premium plan.

use_cases.title: No-code internal database and app

use_cases.title: Self-hosted Airtable alternative

use_cases.title: Workflow automation
use_cases.description: Teams use the Automation Builder to replace manual, spreadsheet-driven processes with triggers and conditional logic.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Baserow?
paragraphs: Baserow is an open-source no-code platform for building databases and applications. Its core product lets teams structure data in Grid, Gallery, Kanban, Calendar, Timeline, Form, and Survey views, and pairs that with an Application Builder for creating front-end apps, an Automation Builder for workflows, and dashboards for visualization.

The core is MIT-licensed and can be self-hosted via Docker, Docker Compose, Helm, or one-click deploys on providers such as Cloudron, Railway, Elestio, Heroku, and DigitalOcean, in addition to Baserow's own cloud-hosted plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Baserow is for
paragraphs: Baserow fits teams looking for an Airtable-style database and app builder but who want the option to self-host for data ownership or compliance reasons, as well as organizations that need HIPAA, GDPR, or SOC 2 Type II compliance on a cloud-hosted no-code platform.

enrichment.short_description: Grist is an open-source spreadsheet-database hybrid that lets teams build no-code apps, forms, and automations from their data.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Grist open-source spreadsheet-database: pricing plans, self-hosting with grist-core, and key formula and access-control features.

features.title: Python-syntax formulas

features.title: Multiple linked views
features.description: Grid, card, calendar, and chart widgets that stay linked to the same underlying data.

features.title: Access rules

features.title: Native forms

Desktop apps available for Linux, macOS, and Windows in addition to web and self-hosted use
Row-, column-, and table-level access rules for granular permission control

faqs.question: Is Grist open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core (grist-core) is released under the Apache License 2.0 and available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can Grist be self-hosted for free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community Edition is free and open source; organizations under $1M in annual revenue can also request free activation keys.

faqs.question: What format does Grist store data in?
faqs.answer: Each Grist document is a self-contained SQLite file.

faqs.question: Does Grist support formulas?
faqs.answer: Yes, formulas can be written in Python syntax and also support common Excel functions.

faqs.question: Is there a Grist desktop app?
faqs.answer: Yes, grist-desktop runs on Linux, macOS, and Windows.

use_cases.title: Team databases with granular permissions
use_cases.description: Organizations that need row- and column-level access control for shared data, such as HR or finance records.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted data workspace

use_cases.title: Formula-driven reporting
use_cases.description: Analysts use Python-syntax formulas to build calculated views and reports directly from raw data.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Grist?
paragraphs: Grist is a relational spreadsheet-database hybrid built by Grist Labs. It combines a familiar spreadsheet grid with Python-syntax formulas, linked card and chart views, native forms, and row/column-level access rules, storing each document as a portable, self-contained SQLite file.

The core, grist-core, is open source under the Apache License 2.0 and can be self-hosted via Docker or run as a desktop app on Linux, macOS, and Windows. Grist Labs also offers a cloud-hosted product with Free, Pro, Business, and Enterprise plans, plus paid self-hosted tiers with added admin and AI features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Grist is for
paragraphs: Grist suits teams that want spreadsheet-like flexibility with database-grade structure and access control, particularly organizations that value data portability (SQLite files), open-source self-hosting, or need row- and column-level permissions that typical spreadsheets don't offer.

enrichment.short_description: SeaTable combines a spreadsheet interface with database power for no-code app building, automation, and team collaboration, hosted in the EU or self-hosted.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SeaTable: no-code spreadsheet-database platform with automation, AI credits, EU cloud hosting, and self-hosted on-premises options.

features.title: Base Editor & multiple views
features.description: Organize data in tables with Gallery, Kanban, Timeline, and Calendar views alongside the standard grid, plus linked records between bases.

features.title: Automation rules & Python scripts
features.description: Build workflows with built-in automation rules and run Python scripts against your data, with usage limits scaling by plan (from 100 script runs/month on Free up to 1,000/user on Enterprise).

features.title: AI-assisted workflows
features.description: Paid plans include monthly AI credits (12.5 on Free up to 500/user on Enterprise) for AI-supported automations, plus a self-hosted AI server option for GDPR-compliant deployments.

features.title: REST API and integrations
features.description: A documented SeaTable API (api.seatable.com) plus native integrations with Zapier, Make, and n8n let teams connect SeaTable to other tools.

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: Choose EU-hosted cloud (certified German data centers), SeaTable Dedicated, or fully self-hosted on-premises server licenses, including a permanently free 3-user on-premises license.

features.title: Templates
features.description: Pre-built, customizable templates help teams start new bases quickly for common use cases.

pros: Multiple deployment options including EU cloud hosting and self-hosted on-premises servers
Generous view types (Gallery, Kanban, Timeline, Calendar) beyond a standard grid
Built-in Python scripting and automation rules for custom workflows
A permanently free 3-user on-premises license is available
Lists large organizations such as the United Nations and Max Planck Society as users, per SeaTable's site
cons: Core server components (e.g., dtable-server) are proprietary, not fully open source
Row, storage, API call, and AI credit limits vary by plan and are limited on the Free tier (10,000 rows, 2GB storage)
Higher-tier on-premises licensing (50+ users) requires contacting sales for custom pricing
Multi-year on-premises license terms are needed to unlock discounted pricing

faqs.question: Is SeaTable open source?
faqs.answer: SeaTable's codebase uses a mix of licenses: some components (dtable-web, dtable-events, thumbnail-server) are Apache License v2, the seaf-server component is AGPLv3, and dtable-server is proprietary — so it is not a single fully open-source stack.

faqs.question: Can SeaTable be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes. SeaTable offers on-premises server licenses, including a permanently free plan for up to 3 users, in addition to its EU cloud and Dedicated Cloud hosting options.

faqs.question: Does SeaTable have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Free cloud plan supports up to 25 users with 10,000 rows, 2GB storage, 3,000 API calls, and 12.5 AI credits.

faqs.question: What automation options does SeaTable support?
faqs.answer: SeaTable includes built-in automation rules and Python script execution, plus integrations with Zapier, Make, and n8n.

faqs.question: Where is SeaTable's data hosted?
faqs.answer: SeaTable's cloud plans are hosted in certified German data centers within the EU; on-premises and Dedicated Cloud options let organizations control data location directly.

use_cases.title: No-code internal app building
use_cases.description: Teams use SeaTable's App Builder and Base Editor to create custom internal tools without writing code.

use_cases.title: Project and workflow tracking
use_cases.description: Kanban, Timeline, and Calendar views combined with automation rules support project management and task tracking.

use_cases.title: GDPR-sensitive data management
use_cases.description: EU-hosted cloud plans and self-hosted on-premises licenses let regulated organizations keep data within Germany or on their own infrastructure.
use_cases.audience: Organizations with strict data residency requirements

features: Unlimited rows, Python runs, API calls, and automations

long_form_blocks.heading: What is SeaTable?
paragraphs: SeaTable is a database and app-building platform that pairs a familiar spreadsheet interface with database-level structure — support for linked records, multiple view types, and unlimited rows within a single base. It is developed by SeaTable GmbH, headquartered in Mainz, Germany, and offers EU cloud hosting alongside self-hosted on-premises deployment.

Beyond data storage, SeaTable includes a no-code App Builder for creating custom applications, built-in automation rules, Python script execution, and paid-tier AI credits for AI-assisted workflows. Its component licensing is mixed: some parts are Apache-2.0 or AGPLv3, while the core dtable-server component is proprietary.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who SeaTable is for
paragraphs: SeaTable suits teams that need database-grade data management without writing SQL, particularly organizations with data-residency requirements that want the option of EU-hosted or fully self-hosted deployment. It's used for internal app building, project tracking, and automation by organizations including the United Nations and the Max Planck Society, per SeaTable's website.

enrichment.short_description: Teable is an open-source, no-code database platform built on Postgres that combines a spreadsheet interface with AI-assisted app and workflow building.

features.title: Postgres-backed no-code database

features.title: Multiple views

features.title: AI fields and AI chat
features.description: Plans include AI-assisted features such as AI fields, AI chat, an app builder for natural-language app generation, and an AI agent called CuppyClaw.

features.title: SQL querying and API access

Community Edition is open source (AGPL-3.0) and self-hostable via Docker
Includes AI-assisted features (AI fields, AI chat, app builder) starting on the free tier
ISO 9001 and ISO 27001 certifications, per Teable's site
Multiple one-click deployment options (Railway, Sealos, Zeabur) in addition to Docker

Headquarters and founding year are not clearly published on the official site

faqs.question: Is Teable open source?
faqs.answer: Teable's Community Edition is licensed under AGPL-3.0 and free to self-host; an Enterprise Edition with additional features is available under a proprietary license.

faqs.question: Can Teable be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community Edition can be self-hosted via Docker, with one-click deployment options for platforms like Railway, Sealos, and Zeabur.

faqs.question: What database does Teable use?
faqs.answer: Teable is built on PostgreSQL, which lets it handle millions of rows — more than typical spreadsheet-style no-code tools.

faqs.question: Does Teable have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Free plan includes 200 credits, 100 automation runs per month, 1,000 rows, and 1GB of attachments per seat.

faqs.question: What AI features does Teable offer?
faqs.answer: Teable includes AI chat, AI-powered fields, a natural-language app builder, and an AI agent called CuppyClaw, available starting on the Free plan.

use_cases.title: No-code app and workflow building
use_cases.description: Teams use Teable's app builder and automation rules to turn spreadsheet-style data into workflows and internal applications without code.

use_cases.title: Large-scale data management
use_cases.description: Because Teable runs on PostgreSQL, it supports datasets with millions of rows that would exceed typical spreadsheet-tool limits.
use_cases.audience: Teams migrating from spreadsheets or legacy systems

use_cases.title: Self-hosted database applications
use_cases.description: Organizations that need to keep data on their own infrastructure can self-host the AGPL-3.0 Community Edition via Docker.
use_cases.audience: Engineering teams with data residency or compliance requirements

features: AI chat, AI field, app builder, CuppyClaw agent access
Additional credits billed at cost with 0% markup
Available for Pro plan and above

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Teable?
paragraphs: Teable is a no-code database platform that pairs a spreadsheet-style grid with a PostgreSQL backend, letting it scale to millions of rows while keeping an Airtable-like editing experience. It offers Grid, Form, Kanban, Gallery, and Calendar views, formulas, filtering, SQL querying, and version history.

The Community Edition is open source under AGPL-3.0 and can be self-hosted via Docker or deployed to platforms like Railway, Sealos, and Zeabur. An Enterprise Edition adds proprietary features such as SSO and custom domains, and Teable's cloud offering layers on AI features — AI fields, AI chat, an app builder, and a credit-based automation system — across Free, Pro, and Business plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Teable is for
paragraphs: Teable fits teams that have outgrown spreadsheet row limits but still want a no-code, spreadsheet-like editing experience, as well as engineering teams that want a self-hostable, Postgres-backed alternative to proprietary no-code database tools.

enrichment.short_description: Mathesar is a free, open-source spreadsheet-like web interface for viewing, editing, and querying PostgreSQL databases without writing SQL.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mathesar: open-source (GPLv3), self-hosted spreadsheet interface for PostgreSQL, maintained by the nonprofit Mathesar Foundation.

features.title: Spreadsheet-like interface for Postgres
features.description: Mathesar lets users view, edit, and query PostgreSQL data through a spreadsheet-style UI without writing SQL.

features.title: Works with native Postgres permissions

features.title: Self-hosted via Docker
features.description: Mathesar installs on your own infrastructure using Docker, so data stays on your own servers.

features.title: Forms and query builders

features.title: Production-ready scalability
features.description: Because it operates directly on Postgres, Mathesar's scalability follows PostgreSQL's own capabilities rather than a separate data layer.

pros: 100% open source (GPLv3) and maintained by the nonprofit Mathesar Foundation, a 501(c)(3)
No pricing tiers or paid lock-in — free to self-host
Works directly on your existing PostgreSQL database using native permissions, not a separate abstraction
Docker-based install described by the project as installable in minutes
cons: Public Beta status — the project describes itself as stable enough for production but has not reached a stable 1.0 release
Requires an existing PostgreSQL database and self-hosting/Docker setup; no first-party managed hosting was confirmed

faqs.question: Is Mathesar free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mathesar is 100% open source under GPLv3 and maintained by the nonprofit Mathesar Foundation, with no paid tiers required to use it.

faqs.question: Do I need my own PostgreSQL database to use Mathesar?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mathesar is a UI layer that connects to and operates directly on an existing PostgreSQL database using Postgres's native schemas and permissions.

faqs.question: How is Mathesar deployed?
faqs.answer: Mathesar is self-hosted using Docker on your own infrastructure, so your data does not leave your own servers.

faqs.question: Is Mathesar production-ready?
faqs.answer: Mathesar is in Public Beta. The project describes it as stable enough for production deployment, but it has not reached a stable 1.0 release.

faqs.question: Who maintains Mathesar?
faqs.answer: Mathesar is maintained by the Mathesar Foundation, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization.

use_cases.title: No-code database administration
use_cases.description: Non-technical staff can view, filter, and edit PostgreSQL data through a spreadsheet-like UI instead of writing SQL.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted data management for regulated teams
use_cases.description: Organizations that must keep data on their own infrastructure can self-host Mathesar via Docker against their existing Postgres instance.
use_cases.audience: Engineering and IT teams with data residency requirements

use_cases.title: Collaborative Postgres data editing
use_cases.description: Teams collaborate on viewing, editing, and querying shared Postgres data using native Postgres permissions for access control.
use_cases.audience: Cross-functional teams sharing a Postgres database

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Mathesar?
paragraphs: Mathesar is an open-source web application that gives PostgreSQL databases a spreadsheet-like interface for viewing, editing, and querying data. It's built to work directly with Postgres's own schemas and permissions rather than introducing a separate data layer, and it's licensed under GPLv3.

The project is maintained by the Mathesar Foundation, a registered 501(c)(3) nonprofit, and is currently in Public Beta. It is self-hosted via Docker so that data remains on the user's own infrastructure, and includes forms, query builders, and relationship mapping for relational data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Mathesar is for
paragraphs: Mathesar is aimed at teams and individuals who want spreadsheet-like ease of use over a real PostgreSQL database — including non-technical users who need to edit data without SQL, and engineering teams that want a free, self-hosted, open-source alternative to proprietary no-code database tools.

features.title: Auto-generated Admin UI

features.title: Fine-grained access control

features.title: Prisma-based migrations

features.title: Session management

features.title: Custom logic via hooks

features.title: TypeScript-first

pros: Free and fully open source under the MIT license, with no vendor lock-in
Auto-generates both a GraphQL API and an admin UI from a single schema definition
Over 1 million npm installs and 250+ contributors, per the project's own site
Deployable to a wide range of hosts (DigitalOcean, Heroku, Render, Vercel, AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) or custom infrastructure
Database migrations handled automatically via Prisma
cons: Requires Node.js development skills — it is a developer framework, not a no-code tool
Keystone 5 is in maintenance mode; only Keystone 6 receives active development
Paid support is only available as a separate enterprise contract through Thinkmill, not a packaged pricing tier
No official managed hosting/cloud product — self-hosting or third-party PaaS deployment is required

faqs.question: Is KeystoneJS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, KeystoneJS is free and open source under the MIT license. Paid enterprise support is available separately through Thinkmill.

faqs.question: What does KeystoneJS generate from my schema?
faqs.answer: Defining a schema in Keystone automatically produces a GraphQL API with CRUD, pagination, sorting, and filtering, plus a customizable admin UI.

faqs.question: What database tooling does KeystoneJS use?
faqs.answer: Keystone uses Prisma to handle database migrations automatically.

faqs.question: Is Keystone 5 still supported?
faqs.answer: Keystone 5 is in maintenance mode; Keystone 6 (published under the @keystone-6/* npm namespace) is the actively developed version.

faqs.question: Who maintains KeystoneJS?
faqs.answer: KeystoneJS is developed and maintained by Thinkmill, an Australian design and development consultancy, with contributions from 250+ people since the project's first commit in 2013.

use_cases.title: Headless CMS for custom front ends
use_cases.description: Developers use Keystone's generated GraphQL API to power custom websites, apps, or storefronts while editors use the auto-generated admin UI.

use_cases.title: Internal admin tools and data APIs
use_cases.description: Because Keystone generates a full CRUD GraphQL API and admin UI from a schema, teams use it to quickly stand up internal data management tools.
use_cases.audience: Engineering teams needing a fast backend and admin UI

long_form_blocks.heading: What is KeystoneJS?
paragraphs: KeystoneJS is a free, open-source (MIT-licensed) headless content management framework for Node.js. Developers define a data schema, and Keystone automatically generates a GraphQL API — with pagination, sorting, filtering, and CRUD operations — along with a customizable admin UI.

Built with TypeScript, GraphQL, and Prisma, Keystone handles database migrations automatically and includes built-in session management, fine-grained access control, and support for custom hooks and GraphQL queries/mutations. The current major version is Keystone 6 (Keystone 5 is in maintenance mode), maintained by Thinkmill with over 1 million npm installs and 250+ contributors since its first commit in 2013.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who KeystoneJS is for
paragraphs: KeystoneJS is built for developers who want a code-first, schema-driven headless CMS rather than a no-code tool — teams that need a customizable GraphQL API and admin UI backing a custom-built front end, and that are comfortable deploying and hosting a Node.js application themselves.

pros: Open source (Apache-2.0) with a self-hostable backend, avoiding vendor lock-in.
Free Tina Cloud tier available for individuals with 2 users.
Content is stored as plain Markdown/MDX/JSON/YAML in Git, preserving full version history.
Visual, in-context editing experience aimed at non-technical content editors.
Active open-source project with over 13,000 GitHub stars and frequent releases.
cons: Editorial workflow and API access are gated behind paid Team Plus/Business Tina Cloud tiers.
AI features on Tina Cloud were still in development at time of review.
Self-hosting the backend requires developer setup of a Git provider, database adapter, and auth provider.
Tina Cloud paid plans are billed per project, which can add up for agencies managing many sites.

faqs.question: Is TinaCMS free?
faqs.answer: TinaCMS's code is open-source under Apache-2.0, and Tina Cloud offers a free plan with 2 users; paid Tina Cloud plans start at $24/month for more users and features.

faqs.question: Does TinaCMS require Tina Cloud?
faqs.answer: No. TinaCMS can be self-hosted using your own database, Git provider, and auth provider instead of the hosted Tina Cloud service.

faqs.question: What content formats does TinaCMS support?
faqs.answer: TinaCMS supports Markdown, MDX, JSON, and YAML content stored in a Git repository.

faqs.question: Who maintains TinaCMS?
faqs.answer: TinaCMS was originally launched by Forestry.io and has been maintained by SSW, an Australian software consultancy, since SSW acquired the project in 2024.

faqs.question: What frameworks does TinaCMS integrate with?
faqs.answer: TinaCMS documents integrations with Next.js, Astro, Hugo, and Jekyll, among other static site generators.

use_cases.title: Developer-led marketing sites
use_cases.description: Teams building Next.js, Astro, or Hugo sites who want Git-based content with a visual editor for marketing content.

use_cases.title: Documentation and blog sites
use_cases.description: Projects storing Markdown/MDX docs or blog posts in Git that need a friendlier editing UI for non-technical writers.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted enterprise content
use_cases.description: Organizations needing full control over their CMS backend and data by self-hosting instead of using Tina Cloud.

features: Up to 3 users (10 max with add-ons)
Up to 5 users (20 max with add-ons)

long_form_blocks.heading: What is TinaCMS?
paragraphs: TinaCMS is an open-source, Git-backed headless CMS that stores content as Markdown, MDX, JSON, or YAML files directly in a Git repository. It combines a GraphQL content API with a visual, in-context editor that lets content authors edit live pages and sync changes back to Git.

The project, originally launched by Forestry.io, has been maintained by SSW, an Australian software consultancy, since SSW acquired TinaCMS in 2024. TinaCMS's backend is Apache-2.0 licensed and can be self-hosted, or run through the hosted Tina Cloud service, which offers a free tier plus paid Team, Team Plus, Business, and Enterprise plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who TinaCMS is for
paragraphs: TinaCMS suits developers and content teams already working with Git-based static sites (Next.js, Astro, Hugo, Jekyll) who want a visual editing layer without giving up version-controlled, file-based content. It also fits organizations wanting to self-host their CMS backend for full data control.

pros: MIT-licensed open-source core that is free forever, with no required account or vendor lock-in.
Fully self-hosted, giving organizations complete control over their data.
Supports both REST and GraphQL APIs, usable with a wide range of frontend and backend stacks.

Pro license renewal (€99/year) is needed to keep receiving updates after the first year.

faqs.question: Is Cockpit CMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Cockpit core is MIT-licensed and free forever; a paid Pro tier (€299 one-time per project, €99/year for continued updates) unlocks additional addons, and custom Enterprise pricing is also available.

faqs.question: Is Cockpit CMS self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cockpit is designed to be installed on your own server and requires PHP 7.3+, PDO with SQLite or MongoDB, and Apache with mod_rewrite.

faqs.question: Does Cockpit CMS support GraphQL?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cockpit offers both REST and GraphQL APIs for content access.

faqs.question: What are Cockpit's Pro addons?
faqs.answer: Paid Pro addons include Autopilot (AI content and image generation), Detektivo (Meilisearch-powered full-text search), Sync (environment-to-environment deployment), Layouts & Pages (visual page building with SEO tools), and Inbox (form submissions).

faqs.question: Who makes Cockpit CMS?
faqs.answer: Cockpit is made by Agentejo, with current development continuing under the Cockpit-HQ organization on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Custom frontend content backends

features: All Pro addons (Autopilot, Detektivo, Sync, Layouts & Pages, Inbox)

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Cockpit CMS?
paragraphs: Cockpit is a self-hosted, API-first headless content management platform made by Agentejo. Its core is MIT-licensed and free to use, letting developers structure content with Collections, Singletons, and Trees, and expose it through REST or GraphQL APIs to any frontend or backend stack.

Beyond the free core, Cockpit offers a one-time-purchase Pro tier (€299 per project, with optional €99/year update renewals) that unlocks addons such as Autopilot (AI content generation), Detektivo (full-text search via Meilisearch), Sync (environment deployment), and Layouts & Pages (visual page building). Custom Enterprise pricing is also available for organizations with specific licensing or support needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Cockpit CMS is for
paragraphs: Cockpit fits developers and organizations that want to self-host their content backend rather than use a SaaS CMS, particularly those with data sovereignty requirements or teams that prefer a one-time license over recurring subscription costs.

faqs.question: Can Statamic run without a database?

faqs.question: Who created Statamic?

use_cases.title: Content-driven marketing sites
