long_form_blocks.heading: What is Statamic?
paragraphs: Statamic is a content management system built on the Laravel PHP framework, launched in 2012 by Jack McDade and Mubashar Iqbal. It can run in flat-file mode, storing content as Markdown and YAML files, or in database mode as projects scale.
Content is structured using Blueprints and a library of 40+ fieldtypes, with the Bard block editor providing flexible, block-based content authoring and Live Preview inside the control panel.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Statamic is for
paragraphs: Statamic suits Laravel and PHP developers and agencies who want a self-hosted CMS with a free Core edition, and who can upgrade to Pro for multi-user roles, revisions, multi-site management, and headless REST/GraphQL delivery.

faqs.question: Is Craft CMS free?

faqs.question: Is Craft CMS open source?
faqs.answer: Craft CMS's source code is publicly viewable on GitHub, but it is distributed under a proprietary Pixel & Tonic license rather than a standard open-source license.

faqs.question: What powers Craft CMS?
faqs.answer: Craft CMS uses Twig templates and a GraphQL API, and is built on the Yii PHP framework.

faqs.question: Does Craft CMS offer hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, Craft Cloud offers managed hosting starting at $120/month per project for the Team plan and $240/month for Pro.

faqs.question: Who makes Craft CMS?
faqs.answer: Craft CMS is made by Pixel & Tonic, Inc., headquartered in Bend, Oregon, USA. Craft 1.0 shipped in 2013.

use_cases.title: Multi-site and multilingual publishing
use_cases.description: Craft's localization tools support unlimited sites and locales from one install.

use_cases.title: Custom-built marketing sites
use_cases.description: Twig templating and a content-first structure let developers build tailored frontends.

features: Unlimited content types, entries, pages, locales

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Craft CMS?
paragraphs: Craft CMS is a content management system built by Pixel & Tonic, headquartered in Bend, Oregon. Craft 1.0 shipped in 2013 after being previewed under the working title 'Blocks CMS'.

It is built with the Yii framework and uses Twig templates or a GraphQL API for decoupled, headless implementations, with multi-site and localization support including an admin interface translated into 25 languages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Craft CMS is for
paragraphs: Craft CMS suits developers and agencies who want a self-hosted, content-first CMS with a free Solo edition for single-user projects, and paid Team/Pro editions or Craft Cloud hosting for multi-user teams and larger deployments.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Grav is an open-source, MIT-licensed flat-file CMS built on PHP 8.3+ and Symfony 7, requiring no database.

features.title: Flat-file architecture

features.title: SvelteKit admin panel

features.title: Built-in MCP server

features.title: Built-in internationalization
features.description: Multi-language support is built into the core.

features.title: Performance caching
features.description: Aggressive caching is designed to deliver sub-100ms page loads.

pros: Completely free and MIT-licensed open source
No database required, content is stored as version-controllable files
Modern technology stack (PHP 8.3+, Symfony 7, SvelteKit-based admin)
Large plugin and theme ecosystem, with 15,571+ GitHub stars
Built-in MCP server for AI agent integration
Used by organizations including Microsoft, Xbox, Cisco, and the USDA
cons: Core project is primarily led by a small team (Trilby Media/Andy Miller) with community and sponsor support
Premium themes (e.g., Helios at $50) and the YetiSearch Pro search plugin are paid add-ons outside the free core

faqs.question: Is Grav free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Grav's core is MIT-licensed and free. Some premium themes and plugins, such as the Helios theme and YetiSearch Pro, are sold separately.

faqs.question: Does Grav use a database?
faqs.answer: No, Grav stores content as Markdown and YAML files instead of using a database.

faqs.question: What powers Grav?
faqs.answer: Grav runs on PHP 8.3+, Symfony 7, and Twig 3, with a SvelteKit-based admin panel.

faqs.question: Does Grav support AI or agent integration?
faqs.answer: Yes, Grav includes a built-in Model Context Protocol (MCP) server for AI agent access.

faqs.question: Who created Grav?
faqs.answer: Grav was created by Andy Miller and is maintained under Trilby Media, with the project dating to 2014.

use_cases.title: Documentation sites
use_cases.description: Grav's flat-file, Git-friendly structure and speed suit technical documentation, and is used for this purpose by organizations like Microsoft and Cisco.

use_cases.title: Version-controlled blogs and websites
use_cases.description: Storing content as files lets teams manage sites with standard Git workflows.
use_cases.audience: Developers who want Git-based content workflows

use_cases.title: Fast, database-free marketing sites
use_cases.description: Sub-100ms cached page performance suits lightweight marketing and brochure sites.

features: Optional premium themes such as Helios

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Grav?
paragraphs: Grav is a flat-file content management system created by Andy Miller, with the project dating to 2014 and maintained under Trilby Media. It stores content as Markdown and YAML files rather than in a database, and is released under the MIT license.

The current stack runs on PHP 8.3+, Symfony 7, and Twig 3, with a SvelteKit 5 and Tailwind 4 based admin panel offering real-time collaborative editing, plus a built-in REST API and Model Context Protocol (MCP) server for AI agent access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Grav is for
paragraphs: Grav suits developers and teams who want a free, self-hosted, database-free CMS with Git-friendly version control, fast cached page delivery, and an extensible plugin/theme ecosystem, including organizations building documentation sites.

Marketplace offers hundreds of plugins and themes for extending functionality.

faqs.question: Is October CMS free?
faqs.answer: New accounts get a free, full-featured license for the first year. After that, continuing to receive updates requires a paid license starting at $39/year for a single site, or an Unlimited Licenses subscription for agencies.

faqs.question: What is October CMS built on?
faqs.answer: It is built on the Laravel PHP framework.

faqs.question: Can October CMS be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, October CMS is self-hosted software that you install and run on your own server.

faqs.question: Is October CMS open source?

use_cases.title: Freelance and small-business sites

features: One year of system updates included
Full feature set for personal or commercial use
One year of system updates included
Covers one production or non-production site

long_form_blocks.heading: What is October CMS?
paragraphs: October CMS is a self-hosted content management system built on the Laravel PHP framework. Its source code is published on GitHub, and new accounts include a free, full-featured license for the first year.

Continued use of the platform after year one relies on a licensing system: a single-site license renews at $39/year, or agencies can use an Unlimited Licenses subscription for multiple projects. A Marketplace offers hundreds of plugins and themes to extend a site's functionality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who October CMS is for
paragraphs: October CMS suits developers and agencies already working in the Laravel/PHP ecosystem who want a self-hosted CMS with a plugin marketplace, rather than a fully managed SaaS platform.

Built-in multi-language support without requiring third-party plugins.
Designed for long-term stability; ProcessWire states sites can run "for years without maintenance or updates" if needed.
Unlimited-depth hierarchical page tree gives flexible content structuring.
cons: Smaller ecosystem than mainstream CMS platforms, with about 1.1k GitHub stars on the core processwire/processwire repo.
Some advanced capabilities (ProCache, Form Builder, PAGEGRID Page Builder) require paid Pro Shop modules.
API-first design requires PHP development knowledge, making it less turnkey for non-developers.

faqs.question: Is ProcessWire free?
faqs.answer: Yes, ProcessWire's core CMS and framework have been free and open source since 2010. Optional Pro modules are sold separately through the Pro Shop.

faqs.question: What license does ProcessWire use?
faqs.answer: Most of the core is licensed under Mozilla Public License 2.0, with some components under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Does ProcessWire support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, multi-language support, including language packs and translatable fields, is built into the core.

faqs.question: Who created ProcessWire?
faqs.answer: ProcessWire was founded in 2010; Ryan Cramer is credited as the author of its core modules.

faqs.question: Can ProcessWire be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, ProcessWire is self-hosted software that you install and run on your own server.

use_cases.title: Developer-built custom sites
use_cases.description: Developers who want full control over markup and data structure can use ProcessWire's API-first approach.

use_cases.title: Multi-language websites
use_cases.description: Built-in multi-language fields and language packs make it suited for sites that need translated content.

use_cases.title: Long-term, low-maintenance sites
use_cases.description: ProcessWire states its sites can run for years without required updates, suiting owners who want minimal ongoing maintenance.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is ProcessWire?
paragraphs: ProcessWire is a free, open-source content management system and web application framework built for developers who want direct API access to content, alongside an editor-friendly admin interface.

Its core has been free since 2010 and is licensed mainly under Mozilla Public License 2.0. Optional paid modules, such as ProCache, Form Builder, and PAGEGRID Page Builder, are available through the ProcessWire Pro Shop.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who ProcessWire is for
paragraphs: ProcessWire suits PHP developers who want a lightweight, API-first framework with a hierarchical content model, including for multi-language sites and long-lived, low-maintenance deployments.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Concrete CMS: an open-source, MIT-licensed CMS for teams, with role-based editing and workflow approvals, plus hosted plans from $4.99/month.

features.title: In-context editing
features.description: WYSIWYG editing directly on the live page rather than a separate back-end form.

features.title: Role-based permissions
features.description: Access control down to individual content blocks.

features.title: Workflow approvals and versioning
features.description: Built-in approval workflows, version history, and change logs for content changes.

features.title: Open-source core

pros: Open-source core licensed under MIT, so it can be self-hosted for free.
Long track record: development began in 2003, originally released as concrete5, giving it a mature codebase.
Granular role-based permissions down to individual content blocks.
Built-in workflow approvals, version control, and change logs support team collaboration.
Compliance-oriented hosting options (SOC 2, HIPAA) are available on paid plans, per Concrete CMS's site.
Concrete CMS states it is used by hundreds of thousands of organizations, including Fortune 500 companies and the U.S. Military.
cons: Entry-level paid hosted plan (Starter) has low included limits: 1 editor, 10,000 monthly page views, 2GB storage.
The self-managed enterprise tier (Custom SLA) requires custom quoting rather than published pricing.
Smaller market share and plugin/theme ecosystem than the largest CMS platforms.

faqs.question: Is Concrete CMS free?
faqs.answer: Its core is open source under the MIT license and can be self-hosted for free. Concrete CMS also sells paid hosted plans starting at $4.99/month (Starter, billed annually).

faqs.question: What was Concrete CMS previously called?
faqs.answer: It was originally released as concrete5, with development starting in 2003, before the branding was updated to Concrete CMS.

faqs.question: Who maintains Concrete CMS?
faqs.answer: PortlandLabs, based in Portland, Oregon.

faqs.question: Does Concrete CMS offer enterprise support?
faqs.answer: Yes, a Custom SLA tier with quote-based enterprise pricing is available.

faqs.question: What compliance options does Concrete CMS hosting offer?
faqs.answer: Concrete CMS states its hosting can offer SOC 2 and HIPAA-compliant options, and describes its security as ISO 27001 certified.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted enterprise sites
use_cases.description: Organizations needing full infrastructure control can self-host the open-source, MIT-licensed core.

use_cases.title: Managed team collaboration
use_cases.description: Teams use paid hosted plans for role-based editing, workflow approvals, and version history.

use_cases.title: Compliance-sensitive sites
use_cases.description: Hosting plans with SOC 2 and HIPAA-compliant options suit regulated industries.

features: Self-managed deployment via GitLab with custom CI/CD pipelines

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Concrete CMS?
paragraphs: Concrete CMS is an open-source content management system for teams, originally released as concrete5 with development beginning in 2003. Its core is licensed under MIT and can be downloaded and self-hosted for free.

Concrete CMS, maintained by PortlandLabs in Portland, Oregon, also sells paid hosted plans starting with a Starter tier at $4.99/month, a Business tier at $19/month, and a quote-based Custom SLA enterprise tier that supports self-managed deployment via GitLab CI/CD.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Concrete CMS is for
paragraphs: Concrete CMS suits teams that want in-context editing, role-based permissions down to the block level, and workflow approvals, whether self-hosting the open-source core or using a paid hosted plan with compliance-oriented options.

enrichment.short_description: TYPO3 is a free, open-source enterprise CMS (GPL v2) with native multi-site and multi-language support, popular across Europe.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: TYPO3: a free, open-source GPL v2 enterprise CMS with built-in multi-site and multi-language support, widely used in Europe.

features.title: Multi-site & multi-language core
features.description: A single TYPO3 installation can run many websites in many languages, sharing templates and resources without extra modules.

features.title: Backend translations
features.description: The admin backend can be translated into 40+ languages via the community-maintained Crowdin translation project.

features.title: Long-Term Support (LTS) releases
features.description: TYPO3 offers LTS release cycles with several years of stability, security, and maintenance, plus optional Extended LTS (ELTS).

features.title: Extension ecosystem
features.description: Functionality can be extended through the TYPO3 Extension Repository (TER).

features.title: Democratic open governance
features.description: The nonprofit TYPO3 Association (1,000+ members) governs the project via a General Assembly voting process.

features.title: Enterprise support network
features.description: TYPO3 GmbH and a network of certified Solution and Enterprise partners provide paid implementation, training, and SLA-backed support.

pros: Fully free and open source under GPL v2, with no licensing fees for the core CMS
Native multi-site/multi-language architecture built into the core
LTS release cycles (plus optional Extended LTS) suited to long-running enterprise projects
Backed by a formal nonprofit association and a global certified-partner network
Widely adopted by government and public-sector organizations in Europe
cons: Steeper learning curve than simpler CMS platforms given its enterprise feature set
Pricing for enterprise support, certification, and partner services is not published and requires direct contact
Backend language packs are community-maintained, so translation completeness varies by language

faqs.question: Is TYPO3 free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the TYPO3 core CMS is free and open source, released under the GNU General Public License v2 (or later).

faqs.question: Who maintains TYPO3?
faqs.answer: The nonprofit TYPO3 Association, based in Baar, Switzerland, governs the project. TYPO3 GmbH, based in Düsseldorf, Germany and fully owned by the Association, provides commercial services.

faqs.question: Does TYPO3 support multiple languages and sites?
faqs.answer: Yes, TYPO3 has built-in support for running many websites and languages from a single installation.

faqs.question: How old is TYPO3?
faqs.answer: TYPO3 was first created in 1997 by Danish developer Kasper Skårhøj.

faqs.question: Is paid enterprise support available?
faqs.answer: Yes, through TYPO3 GmbH and its network of certified TYPO3 partners, though pricing for these services is quote-based rather than publicly listed.

use_cases.title: Government and public-sector websites
use_cases.description: Built-in access controls, LTS release cycles, and multi-language support suit agencies managing large, regulated web presences.

use_cases.title: Multinational corporate websites
use_cases.description: A single installation can serve many country- and language-specific sites from shared content and templates.
use_cases.audience: Large enterprises with multi-country web presence

use_cases.title: Universities and large institutions
use_cases.description: Handles complex site structures and long-term maintenance needs typical of large institutional web portfolios.

features: Community support via forums and Slack

long_form_blocks.heading: What is TYPO3?
paragraphs: TYPO3 is a free, open-source content management system first created in 1997 by Danish developer Kasper Skårhøj. It is released under the GNU General Public License v2 (or later) and governed by the nonprofit TYPO3 Association, with commercial services provided by TYPO3 GmbH.

The CMS has native, built-in support for running multiple websites and multiple languages from a single installation, and offers Long-Term Support release cycles aimed at organizations that need years of stability without recurring licensing fees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who TYPO3 is for
paragraphs: TYPO3 is best suited to organizations with professional web teams managing complex, multi-site, or multi-language projects, such as government agencies, universities, and multinational enterprises, that need a mature, self-hosted CMS backed by a certified-partner support network.

features.title: Umbraco Cloud

features.title: Add-ons suite

features.title: Localized backoffice

pros: Core CMS is free, open source (MIT license), and self-hostable with no licensing fees
Managed Umbraco Cloud has published, transparent pricing starting at €45/month
Backoffice interface localized into 20+ languages
Runs on .NET / ASP.NET Core, fitting into Microsoft-based infrastructure
Used by well-known brands including Domino's, Volvo, and Heineken, per umbraco.com
cons: Higher Umbraco Cloud tiers (Professional at €730/month, Enterprise custom) are priced well above small-site budgets
Some official add-ons, such as Umbraco Forms and Umbraco Deploy, require separate commercial licenses on top of the core CMS
Self-hosting requires .NET hosting knowledge, unlike simpler PHP-based CMS options

faqs.question: Is Umbraco free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Umbraco CMS is open source under the MIT license and free to self-host.

faqs.question: What is Umbraco Cloud?
faqs.answer: A managed hosting service on Microsoft Azure with published plans starting at €45/month (Starter) and going up to custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Who founded Umbraco?
faqs.answer: Niels Hartvig founded Umbraco, first released in 2005.

faqs.question: What languages is the Umbraco backoffice available in?
faqs.answer: Umbraco ships with backoffice translations for more than 20 languages, including English, German, French, Spanish, and Japanese.

faqs.question: Was Umbraco acquired?
faqs.answer: Umbraco A/S was acquired by growth investor Monterro in 2021, according to a Business Wire announcement.

use_cases.title: Enterprise marketing websites

use_cases.title: Agency-built client sites

use_cases.title: .NET development teams
use_cases.description: Development teams already working in the Microsoft/.NET ecosystem can build on Umbraco without adopting a new stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Umbraco?
paragraphs: Umbraco is an open-source .NET content management system founded by Niels Hartvig in 2005 and headquartered in Odense, Denmark. The core CMS is released under the MIT license and can be self-hosted for free.

Alongside the free open-source core, Umbraco offers Umbraco Cloud, a managed hosting service on Microsoft Azure with published pricing plans ranging from a €45/month Starter tier to custom Enterprise pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Umbraco is for
paragraphs: Umbraco suits organizations and agencies already working in the .NET ecosystem that want a flexible, self-hostable CMS with the option to move to fully managed cloud hosting as needs grow, from marketing teams to enterprise development teams.

Straightforward upgrade path for existing Drupal 7 sites
Runs well on modest or shared hosting
1,300+ community modules and add-ons available
Backed by nonprofit Software Freedom Conservancy membership
cons: Smaller ecosystem than Drupal or WordPress, with roughly 3,600 known sites and under 3,000 community members
No official managed cloud hosting product; users must self-host or arrange third-party hosting
As a Drupal 7-era fork, it does not carry forward newer Drupal 8+/9+ core features and modules

faqs.question: Is Backdrop CMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Backdrop CMS is entirely free and open source under the GPL version 2 (or later) license, with no paid plans.

faqs.question: What is Backdrop CMS based on?
faqs.answer: It was forked from Drupal 7 in 2013 by Drupal developers Nate Lampton and Jen Lampton.

faqs.question: Can I upgrade a Drupal 7 site to Backdrop?
faqs.answer: Yes, Backdrop CMS provides a built-in upgrade path from Drupal 7.

faqs.question: Who governs Backdrop CMS?
faqs.answer: A community Project Management Committee (PMC) modeled on Apache Software Foundation governance; Backdrop is a member project of the Software Freedom Conservancy.

faqs.question: How many sites use Backdrop CMS?
faqs.answer: The Backdrop CMS website reports more than 3,600 known sites running the platform.

use_cases.title: Budget-conscious nonprofit and community sites
use_cases.description: Backdrop targets organizations like nonprofits, schools, museums, and libraries that need Drupal 7-style functionality without expensive upkeep.

use_cases.title: Drupal 7 migration path
use_cases.description: Sites still running end-of-life Drupal 7 can move to Backdrop using its built-in upgrade path while retaining familiar architecture.

use_cases.title: Small business and personal websites
use_cases.description: Runs efficiently on shared/budget hosting for smaller sites and blogs.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Backdrop CMS?
paragraphs: Backdrop CMS is a free, open-source content management system written in PHP and forked from Drupal 7 in 2013 by developers Nate Lampton and Jen Lampton. It is released under the GNU General Public License v2 (or later).

Backdrop retains much of Drupal 7's functionality and architecture while offering a built-in upgrade path from Drupal 7, more than 1,300 modules and themes, and community governance through a Project Management Committee. It is a member project of the Software Freedom Conservancy.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Backdrop CMS is for
paragraphs: Backdrop CMS is aimed at organizations and individuals who want the feature set of Drupal 7 without expensive upkeep, including nonprofits, schools, museums, libraries, small businesses, and existing Drupal 7 site owners looking to migrate off an end-of-life platform.

faqs.question: Is Silverstripe CMS free?

faqs.question: Who created Silverstripe?

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Silverstripe CMS?
paragraphs: Silverstripe CMS is a free, open-source content management system and PHP web framework founded in 2000 in Wellington, New Zealand, by Sam Minnée, Sigurd Magnusson, and Tim Copeland. The core software is released under the BSD License.

Silverstripe.org reports more than 50,000 live Silverstripe CMS installations and 2,500+ community modules. Commercial backer Silverstripe Limited also operates Silverstripe Cloud, a quote-priced managed hosting platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Silverstripe CMS is for
paragraphs: Silverstripe CMS is designed for developers, marketers, content authors, and IT managers, and is commonly used by agencies and organizations, including government and enterprise clients, that need a customizable, security-focused PHP CMS.

enrichment.short_description: Publii is a free, open-source desktop CMS for building and publishing static websites without server-side hosting requirements.

Premium themes and plugins in the marketplace cost extra

faqs.question: Is Publii free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Publii desktop app is free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license. Optional premium themes and plugins are sold separately in its marketplace.

faqs.question: What platforms does Publii run on?
faqs.answer: Publii is a desktop application available for Windows 10+, macOS 12+, and 64-bit Linux.

faqs.question: Where can I host a site built with Publii?
faqs.answer: Publii can deploy to GitHub Pages, GitLab, Netlify, Amazon S3, Google Cloud, SFTP/FTPS, or manually export files.

faqs.question: Is Publii open source?
faqs.answer: Yes. Publii is built with Electron and Vue.js and its source code is available on GitHub under the GPL-3.0 license.

use_cases.title: Static blogging without a server

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Publii?
paragraphs: Publii is a free, open-source static site CMS delivered as a desktop application for Windows, macOS, and Linux. Rather than running on a web server, Publii lets you write and manage content locally, then build and publish a static website to a host of your choosing.

The app includes built-in tools for SEO (structured data, Open Graph, XML sitemaps) and privacy compliance (GDPR/CCPA cookie banners), along with support for deploying to GitHub Pages, GitLab, Netlify, Amazon S3, Google Cloud, or SFTP/FTPS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Publii is for
paragraphs: Publii suits bloggers, writers, and small businesses who want a traditional CMS-style editing experience without paying for server hosting or managing site security. It also appeals to developers who want a free, open-source way to generate and deploy static sites to platforms like GitHub Pages or Netlify.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Micro.blog?
paragraphs: Micro.blog is a blogging platform, founded in 2017 by Manton Reece, that lets users publish short posts, essays, photo blogs, and email newsletters at their own domain name. It integrates with the fediverse and IndieWeb standards and automatically cross-posts content to networks like Mastodon, Threads, and Bluesky.

Pricing starts at $5/month for the base plan and scales up through Premium ($10/month), Family ($15/month), and Studio ($20/month), each adding capabilities such as multiple blogs, newsletters, multi-user collaboration, and podcast/video hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Micro.blog is for
paragraphs: Micro.blog fits independent writers who want to own their content at a custom domain while staying connected to the fediverse, as well as families or small groups who want to co-manage blogs, and podcasters who need built-in recording and hosting through the Studio plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Blot?
paragraphs: Blot is a hosted publishing tool that turns a folder of files into a website. Instead of writing posts inside a web-based CMS, users add or edit files in a folder using their own text editor, and Blot publishes the changes automatically.

The service costs $6 per month per site, with that price covering hosting, all available templates, custom domain support, and SSL certificates. Blot has operated for over a decade and offers documentation, example sites, and developer resources.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Blot is for
paragraphs: Blot is aimed at writers and developers who'd rather work in their existing text editor and file system than a browser-based CMS, including people looking to publish an existing folder of notes or documents as a public website.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Bear Blog?
paragraphs: Bear Blog is a free, open-source blogging platform built around simplicity and speed. Pages contain no trackers, ads, or JavaScript, and average roughly 2.7kb in size, with the project's source code publicly available on GitHub.
It includes custom domain support, RSS and Atom feeds, and a set of free themes, positioning itself against feature-heavy, script-laden blogging platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Bear Blog is for

paragraphs: Shopware is a German ecommerce platform offering a free, open-source Community Edition (MIT licensed) alongside paid SaaS and PaaS plans. It includes an integrated CMS, SEO and analytics tools, a Flow Builder for automation, and B2B-specific features such as digital sales rooms and dynamic access rules.

Paid plans start with Rise from €600/month, scale to Evolve from €2,400/month, and top out with the custom-priced Beyond tier, each adding support levels, B2B capabilities, and access to features like the Shopware Intelligence Copilot.
